perturbation.original_text: Institution shall protect Discloser’s Confidential Information using not less than the same care it uses with respect to its own confidential information, but at all times at least reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Institution shall endeavor to protect Discloser’s Confidential Information using similar care as it uses with respect to its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'shall protect' to 'shall endeavor to protect' and removes the 'at all times at least reasonable care' clause, making the obligation to protect confidential information less stringent and more subjective. 'Similar care' is also ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to 'endeavor to protect' with 'similar care', the modified text introduces ambiguity and might not meet the standard of 'reasonable efforts' required under Michigan law. The deletion of 'at all times at least reasonable care' directly contradicts the baseline of reasonable care.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'shall protect' with 'shall endeavor to protect' and removes 'at all times at least reasonable care'. The original text provides a clear obligation to protect confidential information with at least reasonable care. The changed text weakens this obligation, creating ambiguity regarding the level of care required. The Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA), as indicated by the Law Explanation, requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain trade secret secrecy. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly state 'reasonable efforts,' it discusses misappropriation and injunctions, implying a standard of protection. The modification introduces ambiguity and potentially falls short of 'reasonable efforts' which would violate the need to prevent misappropriation. Therefore, the modification creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Institution may disclose the Confidential Information to the extent that such disclosure is required by law or court order, provided, however, that Institution promptly provides to Discl oser p rior written not ice of suc h disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Institution may disclose the Confidential Information if disclosure is believed to be required by law or court order, and Institution will attempt to provide Discloser with prior notice of such disclosure when practical.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is required' to 'is believed to be required' introduces a subjective element. Changing 'promptly provides prior written notice' to 'will attempt to provide...when practical' makes the notification requirement less certain. This allows Institution to determine if disclosure is needed, which could jeopardize confidentiality, and removes urgency of the notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45 dictates the requirements for subpoenas, which are court orders that may require the disclosure of confidential information. While the rule allows for objections to a subpoena based on privilege or undue burden, it does not allow a party to unilaterally determine that disclosure is 'believed' to be required. Furthermore, changing the notification requirement to 'attempt to provide when practical' could violate the due process rights afforded to the disclosing party, preventing them from challenging the subpoena in a timely manner. The notice should be prompt, so Discloser can have a chance to respond.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and reduces the certainty of required actions, potentially contradicting legal obligations. The original text states the Institution 'is required' to disclose confidential information and 'promptly provides prior written notice.' The modified text changes this to 'is believed to be required' introducing subjectivity, and 'will attempt to provide...when practical' making the notification requirement less certain. The Law Explanation notes this could violate due process rights. Snippet 1 describes the requirements for a subpoena, and the Law Explanation connects the changes to FRCP 45, implying the changes could hinder the Discloser's ability to challenge the subpoena in a timely manner. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists because the altered text weakens legally mandated obligations, creating potential non-compliance and hindering due process.

perturbation.original_text: Institution shall promptly return o r, at Discloser’s option, destr oy, all Discloser’s Confidential Information related to a clinical study if Institution does not participate in such clinical study, upon termination or expiration of this Agreement, or upon written req uest by th e Discl oser, provided however, that Institution may retain in confidence under this Agreement one archived copy of the Discloser’s Confidential Information solely for the purpose of administering In stitution’s obligations hereun der.
perturbation.changed_text: Institution will endeavor to return or destroy Discloser’s Confidential Information related to a clinical study if Institution does not participate in such clinical study, upon termination or expiration of this Agreement, or upon written request by the Discloser, provided that Institution may retain one copy of the Discloser’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly return or destroy' to 'will endeavor to return or destroy' makes the return/destruction of confidential information less mandatory. Removing 'at Discloser's option' regarding destruction gives Institution discretion, and removing 'solely for the purpose of administering Institution’s obligations hereunder' removes purpose of retaining the copy.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR Part 11 outlines requirements for electronic records and electronic signatures within the pharmaceutical and medical device industries. If the 'Confidential Information' includes electronic records related to clinical studies, the regulation requires appropriate controls to ensure data integrity, audit trails, and record retention. By changing 'shall promptly return or destroy' to the much weaker 'will endeavor to return or destroy', and by removing specificity as to purpose of retention, the modified text introduces ambiguity that could lead to non-compliance with Part 11. In particular, 21 CFR § 11.10(a) states that systems must have controls to ensure accurate and reliable records, and ambiguous destruction policies make that harder to prove. The institution is able to now keep a copy for unspecified reasons. If the institution fails to maintain control over such copies, then a violation would occur.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall promptly return or destroy' to 'will endeavor to return or destroy' introduces ambiguity regarding the mandatory nature of returning or destroying confidential information. The scraped law snippet from 21 CFR Part 11 emphasizes controls for accurate and reliable records. The modified text weakens the obligation, creating a situation where compliance with 21 CFR Part 11 could be jeopardized. The removal of 'at Discloser's option' and specificity regarding the purpose of retaining a copy further exacerbates this issue, as it grants the institution more discretion without a clear framework for data integrity as required by the regulation.

perturbation.original_text: Institution agrees not to disclose or ot herwise make available any of the Confidential Information to anyone except those employees and agents of Institution who need to know the Confidential Information to carry out the Purpose and who are bound by obligations of non- use and non -disclosure substantially similar to those set forth herein . Institution shall be responsible for any disclosure or use of the Confidential Information by its employees or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: Institution will not intentionally disclose or make available any of the Confidential Information to anyone except employees and agents of Institution who may need to access the Confidential Information, provided those parties are generally informed of the need for confidentiality. Institution will endeavor to address breaches of confidentiality by its employees or agents.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'intentionally' before disclose, it implies unintentional disclosure is acceptable. Replacing 'need to know' with 'may need to access' broadens the scope of permissible access. Changing 'bound by obligations...substantially similar' to 'generally informed of the need' weakens the confidentiality requirement. Replacing 'shall be responsible' with 'will endeavor to address breaches' reduces accountability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: If the 'Confidential Information' includes Protected Health Information (PHI) as defined by HIPAA, the modifications weaken the protections required by law. Specifically, 45 CFR § 164.502(a) requires covered entities to implement reasonable safeguards to protect PHI from unauthorized disclosure. The 'intentionally' qualifier, the broadened access scope, the weakened confidentiality requirement, and the reduced accountability contradict this requirement. While this NDA is not directly related to health services, the potential inclusion of PHI in clinical trial data means that HIPAA still applies. The covered entities must ensure that any Business Associates (the Institution, in this case) have adequate safeguards in place. Making these restrictions optional could lead to a violation of the Privacy Rule.

All covered entities, except "small health plans," must have been compliant with the Privacy Rule by April 14, 2003. 90 Small health plans, however, had until April 14, 2004 to comply. Small Health Plans. A health plan with annual receipts of not more than $5 million is a small health plan. 91 Health plans that file certain federal tax returns and report receipts on those returns should use the guidance provided by the Small Business Administration at 13 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 121.104 to calculate annual receipts. Health plans that do not report receipts to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), for example, group health plans regulated by the Employee Retirement Income Security Act 1974 (ERISA) that are exempt from filing income tax returns, should use proxy measures to determine their annual receipts. 92 See What constitutes a small health plan? In addition to the removal of the above-stated identifiers, the covered entity may not
perturbation.justification: The changes in the modified text introduce ambiguities and weaken the confidentiality obligations, potentially contradicting HIPAA regulations regarding the protection of Protected Health Information (PHI). The original text mandates strict non-disclosure obligations, while the changed text uses terms like 'intentionally disclose,' 'may need to access,' and 'generally informed,' which could lead to unauthorized disclosures of PHI. 45 CFR § 164.502(a) requires covered entities to implement reasonable safeguards to protect PHI from unauthorized disclosure, and the modifications weaken these protections. The original contract mandated responsibility for any disclosure, whereas the changed contract says the institution 'will endeavor to address breaches' which weakens accountability. The modifications reduce accountability for breaches, potentially failing to meet the required safeguards under HIPAA.
